turns.content: I am building a tool to automate file parsing, but I keep hitting compile errors when trying to access two fields at once. Why does the compiler even allow me to borrow distinct fields of a struct simultaneously? It feels like it should just be impossible.

That is actually one of Rust's most powerful features called borrowing split, which lets you reference multiple different parts of the same value at once. The language model will allow this because it ensures that no two mutable references point to overlapping data within a single struct instance.
But if I can do that, why am I still getting errors? It seems like the compiler is being overly restrictive and preventing me from accessing both fields in my parsing logic.

The issue usually stems from how your struct methods are defined. If you define a method that takes &self, it borrows the entire struct by reference, which prevents you from borrowing specific fields independently. You need to use split-borrowing patterns instead.
I tried using references on individual fields but the compiler still complains about overlapping borrows. Is there a way to tell Rust I only want to borrow certain parts without borrowing everything?

You can achieve this by explicitly naming the references in your function signature rather than using &self. This tells the compiler exactly which fields you are borrowing, allowing it to track ownership correctly and prevent unintended overlaps.

Ah, I see now. By specifying each field explicitly as a reference parameter, I can avoid the whole-struct borrow issue entirely. This means my parsing tool will be able to access both fields without triggering any ownership conflicts.

Exactly. When you name the individual references in your function signature, you give Rust precise control over what is being borrowed, which resolves the conflict and lets your automation tool run smoothly.
